sources.notes: Read the complete English statement, all three examples, and limits: one tree with 2 <= n <= 1000000, 1 <= m <= 1000000000, 5 seconds, 768 MB, and answers modulo 998244353. Path length means its number of vertices.

Read the rendered full 2039G tutorial and model code, not only the collapsed public index. It gives the per-vertex h_u reduction, the restricted Mobius formula, rough-number and Dirichlet-prefix machinery, the D > 2 sqrt(m) shortcut, and grouped threshold handling. Its sharper final small-case complexity claim explicitly leaves the proof for later.

Actually opened and read the complete Luogu article. It independently recovers the prime-length path reduction, least-prime-factor thresholds, gcd inversion, floor-quotient grouping, and a largest-prime-factor/prime-counting alternative. Its final asymptotic discussion is intentionally vague, so it is used as a mathematical cross-check rather than the source of the recorded complexity.

Read the full independent post and code. It cross-checks the rough-number formula, Du/Min25-style summatory computation, and the diameter half-bound. The prose duplicates the same inequality in both case bullets; the code and official tutorial resolve the second bullet as the complementary small-diameter case.

Read the complete CF2039G section and implementation. It independently shows the two-pass tree DP, compressed floor-quotient sieve arrays, large-diameter prime-only shortcut, and a conservative O(m log n / log m) product-aggregation bound. No accepted verdict was inferred from this blog.

The Codeforces status and submission pages visibly showed Accepted (1234 ms, 53980 KB), and the complete source was read. It independently implements iterative rerooting, compressed prime counting, a dynamic-phi/rough-number split, and restricted Mobius prefix sums, validating the same reduction and summation formula with a refined implementation threshold.

Independently proved the path-to-least-prime-factor equivalence, the D-rough Mobius formula, the restricted Dirichlet recurrence, both diameter cases, and a conservative quotient-state complexity bound. In-memory exhaustive checks covered every labeled tree for 2 <= n <= 5 and every 1 <= m <= 6 (870 tree/m cases); direct path-LCM enumeration, the local threshold test, and the Mobius formula always agreed. Also checked 2900 restricted-prefix states and 21424 valid large-diameter threshold vectors.

annotation.summary: Count assignments a_u in [1,m] on a tree such that for every path with k vertices, k does not divide the LCM of its vertex values, while gcd(a_1,...,a_n)=1.
annotation.constraints: Single test case; 2 <= n <= 1000000, 1 <= m <= 1000000000; the input is a tree; time limit 5 seconds, memory limit 768 MB, modulus 998244353.
annotation.core_idea: For each vertex u, let h_u be the maximum number of vertices on a simple path containing u, and let D=max h_u, the tree diameter in vertices. A valid assignment is equivalent to lpf(a_u)>h_u for every u, with lpf(1)=infinity: if a prime p<=h_u divides a_u, take a p-vertex subpath of a longest path through u; conversely, every vertex on a k-vertex path has h_u>=k, so no prime divisor of k occurs in any path value and k cannot divide the LCM. Define R(x,t)=#{y<=x:lpf(y)>t}. Mobius inversion of the global gcd condition gives Ans=sum_{d<=m} mu(d)[lpf(d)>D] product_u R(floor(m/d),h_u). The restriction on d is exact because some vertex has h_u=D; for D-rough d, writing a_u=d b_u preserves each local roughness condition. Group d by equal floor(m/d). For block coefficients use M_D(x)=sum_{d<=x,lpf(d)>D}mu(d); with Q_D(x)=R(x,D), the restricted convolution mu_D * 1_D = epsilon yields M_D(x)=1-sum_{b=2..x,lpf(b)>D} M_D(floor(x/b)), evaluated by floor-quotient grouping. Compute all R(q,h) and M_D(q) only on the O(sqrt(m)) distinct quotients q=floor(m/i), using Min25/Legendre-phi style prime elimination and a Du-sieve recurrence. Tree geometry supplies h_u>=ceil(D/2). If D>2 floor(sqrt(m)), every locally allowed value is 1 or a prime, so the answer is product_u(1+max(0,pi(m)-pi(h_u)))-max(0,pi(m)-pi(D)). Otherwise replace every h_u by pi(h_u), group equal thresholds, and raise each rough-number count to its frequency before the quotient-block sum.
annotation.complexity: For a compressed floor-quotient implementation, tree rerooting is O(n). Standard prime-count/rough-number and restricted-Mobius preparation is O(m^(3/4) polylog m) or better on O(sqrt(m)) quotient states. All changing threshold primes lie in [ceil(D/2),D], apart from at most one boundary representative that is greater than D/4 by Bertrand's postulate. A threshold p touches O(sqrt(m)) states for p<=sqrt(m) and O(m/p) states otherwise; summing over the relevant primes is O(m/log m), and binary exponentiation adds a factor O(log n). Thus a conservative proved bound is O(n + m^(3/4) polylog m + (m/log m) log n) time and O(n+sqrt(m)) auxiliary memory, with m<=2 handled directly. The official recursive implementation states O(m^(2/3)) for restricted summatory queries and a sharper small-case product bound, but its tutorial omits that sharper proof.
tricks: Use prime-length subpaths through a chosen vertex to turn every path-LCM restriction into a local least-prime-factor threshold.
Treat 1 as having infinite least prime factor so rough-number counts include it uniformly.
Apply Mobius inversion to gcd=1, then observe that only multipliers with no prime factor <=D can divide every assigned value.
Run the Dirichlet-prefix recurrence inside the multiplicative semigroup of D-rough numbers rather than using the ordinary Mertens prefix.
Compress all arithmetic arguments to the distinct values floor(m/i).
Group h_u by pi(h_u), because rough-number counts change only when the threshold crosses a prime.
Use h_u>=ceil(D/2) from diameter endpoints both for the prime-only shortcut and for the product-DP complexity bound.
Aggregate equal threshold groups with modular exponentiation instead of multiplying once per vertex.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains in the local-threshold equivalence, restricted Mobius formula, or large-diameter subtraction after the independent proofs and exhaustive checks.

The official tutorial states a sharper small-diameter bound of roughly O(m/log^2(m) times a logarithmic factor) and says its proof will be added later. This payload deliberately records the weaker O((m/log m) log n) aggregation bound that can be derived directly from the quotient-state implementation.

The Luogu article describes a materially different largest-prime-factor enumeration with soft-O(m^(3/4)) prime-counting support, but omits enough final complexity detail that it is not promoted to a separate reviewed solution variant.
The Sktn0089 post has a duplicated case inequality in prose; this is a source typo, not an algorithmic ambiguity, because its code and the official tutorial use complementary large/small cases.
The accepted submission uses a refined dynamic-phi versus rough-enumeration threshold rather than the tutorial's exact D split. It is implementation corroboration, not a second taxonomy variant.
No new taxonomy tag is proposed: the current tags for tree diameter, rerooting DP, Mobius, Du sieve, and Min25 sieve cover the reusable techniques.
proof_risks: h_u and D count vertices, while rerooting arm lengths usually count edges; h_u is one plus the two largest incident-arm lengths.

For necessity, a p-vertex path containing u is obtained as a contiguous subpath of a longest path through u. It is not always valid to assume another vertex is distance p-1 from u, for example when u is a star center.
For sufficiency, one must use h_u>=k for every vertex u on the chosen k-vertex path; checking only an endpoint or only one prime factor is insufficient.
The Mobius multiplier d must be D-rough, where D=max h_u. Restricting it only by min h_u or forgetting that some diameter vertex has h_u=D gives extra impossible terms.
R(x,t) includes 1. Base cases that return zero when x<=t lose assignments containing value 1.
The prime-only shortcut requires every h_u>floor(sqrt(m)). At equality a square of a prime may still be an allowed composite, so the branch boundary must be handled exactly.
In the prime-only branch, gcd>1 occurs exactly when all vertices receive the same prime p>D. Use max(0,pi(m)-pi(D)) and similarly clip every local prime count when h_u>m.
M_D is not the ordinary Mertens function. In its convolution recurrence, both the divisor and cofactor ranges are restricted to numbers with no prime factor <=D.
A quotient block for q=floor(m/d) is d in [floor(m/(q+1))+1, floor(m/q)]; off-by-one errors corrupt the restricted Mobius coefficient.
The first threshold group may be represented by the largest prime <=min h_u and therefore lie below D/2. Handle it separately; Bertrand's postulate only guarantees it is greater than D/4.
The complexity reduction relies on all other threshold changes lying in the interval induced by h_u in [ceil(D/2),D]. Treating the h_u as arbitrary recreates the prohibitive O(n sqrt(m)) work.
Recursive DFS on a path with n=1000000 can overflow the process stack; an implementation should use iterative rooting/rerooting or explicitly safe stack management.
Restricted Mobius block sums are signed; normalize negative residues only after preserving their integer or modular difference correctly.

solution_variants.name: diameter-threshold-mobius-min25
solution_variants.summary: Compute every h_u by two-pass rerooting, replace the path constraints by h_u-rough value choices, enforce gcd=1 with a D-rough Mobius sum, and evaluate rough counts plus restricted Mobius prefixes on compressed floor quotients. Use the 1-or-prime closed form when D>2 floor(sqrt(m)); otherwise group vertices by pi(h_u) and accumulate the quotient-block sum.
solution_variants.complexity: Conservative quotient-state bound: O(n + m^(3/4) polylog m + (m/log m) log n) time and O(n+sqrt(m)) auxiliary memory; standard implementations can improve the summatory subroutines and the official tutorial claims a sharper small-case bound.

tags.evidence: The decisive structural proof converts the condition k not dividing the LCM on every k-vertex path into the exact local rule lpf(a_u)>h_u by extracting prime-length subpaths and excluding every prime divisor of k.
The global gcd=1 indicator is expanded as sum_{d|all a_u}mu(d), producing the central formula sum_d mu(d)[d is D-rough] product_u R(floor(m/d),h_u).

The algorithm must evaluate prime counts and h-rough counts R(q,h) for large m across all distinct q=floor(m/i); prime-elimination DP on the O(sqrt(m)) quotient states is the Min25-style core that removes dependence on m or n per query.

Each quotient block needs M_D(r)-M_D(l-1), where M_D is the restricted Mobius prefix. It is obtained from mu_D*1_D=epsilon by a memoized floor-division Dirichlet recurrence, exactly the Du-sieve pattern on D-rough numbers.

D=max h_u is the tree diameter and every h_u>=ceil(D/2). This bound is essential both to the D>2 sqrt(m) prime-only branch and to bounding the number and cost of threshold groups in the general branch.
A bottom-up pass computes the two deepest child arms and a top-down pass supplies the best parent-side arm, yielding h_u for all vertices in O(n).
The only global coupling is gcd(a_1,...,a_n)=1; its divisor structure determines the Mobius inversion and the all-equal-prime subtraction in the large-diameter branch.
The solution separates D>2 floor(sqrt(m)), where every allowed value is 1 or prime, from the general rough-number sieve case; the first branch avoids the expensive Mobius product entirely.
